---
title: "Basic Mathematical Concepts"
subtitle: "Learning R with Dr. Hu and His Friends Workshop Series"
author: "Yue Hu"
institute: "Department of Political Science, THU"
---

{{< include _extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

## Outline

1. Basic algebra
2. Vectors
3. Matrices and solving equations

::: {.fragment}

*Research question*: What is the underlying logic of data analysis in R?

:::


## Basic Operations

- R's arithmetic operators match math: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`
- Type an expression directly in the Console to compute

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: algebraBasics

# Four basic operations (add, subtract, multiply, divide)
1 + 2
3 - 4
5 * 6
7 / 8

# Compound expression
(2 + 4) / (5 - 3)
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Whitespace usage" .fragment}

- Whitespace does not affect computation but improves readability
- The Tidyverse style guide recommends consistent spaces and blank lines
- Packages such as `styler` and `air` can unify formatting

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: woSpace

# The line below has irregular spacing — looks messy but R still runs it correctly
(2+4) / (  5-  3            )
```

:::

---

- Power `^`, modulo `%%`, logarithm `log()` / `log10()`, exponential `exp()`
- Common shortcuts: `sqrt()` (square root), `abs()` (absolute value)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: moreAlgebra

10^2
5 %% 3
log(11)
log10(100)
exp(12)

# Two ways to take a square root
9^(1/2)
sqrt(9)

# Absolute value
abs(-9)
```

:::

---

- Trigonometric functions: `sin()`, `cos()`, `tan()`, `acos()`, `asin()`, `atan()`
- `pi` is R's pre-stored constant for π
- Other pre-stored constants: `letters`, `LETTERS`, `month.name`, `month.abb`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: trigFunctions

sin(pi)
cos(pi)
tan(pi / 2)
acos(1)
asin(1)
atan(0.5)

```

:::

::: {.callout-important title="Scientific notation" .fragment}

- `e` connects the coefficient and the exponent, indicating a power of 10
- Example: 200 = 2e2; 0.002 = 2e-3

:::

---

- Boolean algebra: `TRUE` / `FALSE` (or `T` / `F` for short)
- Logical operators: `&` (and), `|` (or), `!` (not)
- The empty object: `NULL`
- Comparison operators: `==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: boolean

# Equalities
1 == 2
1 == TRUE # TRUE / FALSE are 1 and 0 with a logical attribute
0 != F

# Inequalities
1 != 2
1 > 2
1 < 2
1 >= 2
1 <= 2

# Boolean operations

3 > 2 & 2 > 1
12 & 2 > 2
-3 | 2 > 2
```

:::

---

- Boolean precedence comes last: comparisons first, then logical connectives
- A bare value in a Boolean expression triggers a self-equality check

::: {.callout-tip title="Using the equal sign" .fragment}

- `==` expresses equality; `=` is reserved for assignment
- Beginners often misuse `=` for `==`---practice fixes it

:::

## Vectors

- Vector: a sequence of values with a direction
- Three ways to create: `c()`, `:` (consecutive integers), `seq()`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: sequence

# Method 1: enter values one by one with c() (the most basic way)
a <- c(11, 12, 13, 14, 15)
# Method 2: the colon : operator (consecutive integers only)
a1 <- 11:15
# Method 3: seq() for more flexible sequences
a2 <- seq(from = 11, to = 15, by = 1)
a3 <- seq(11, 15)

# Verify all three are identical
a == a1
a == a2
a == a3
```

:::

---

- Vector indexing: `[]` extracts a subset
- `a[2]` picks the 2nd element; `a[c(3,4)]` picks multiple elements

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: vector

# Create a demo vector
a <- c(11, 12, 13, 14, 15)

# Single element (by position index)
a[2]
a[5]

# Multiple elements (position vector inside [])
a[c(3,4)]
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-important title="The assignment operator `<-`" .fragment}

- `<-` is R's most common assignment operator; the arrow means "store into"
- Shortcut: `Alt + -` on Windows / Linux, `Option + -` on Mac
- R is virtually the only popular language using `<-`, a legacy of the APL keyboard layout

:::

---

- Vector addition / subtraction: element-wise on corresponding positions
- Scalar with vector: the scalar applies to every element

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: vectorAdd

a <- c(11, 12, 13, 14, 15)

# Vector with scalar
b <- a + 4
c <- a - 0.5

# Vector with vector
a + b
a - c
```

:::

---

- Element-wise (Hadamard) product: `*`, multiply corresponding positions
- Inner (dot) product: `%*%`, returns a scalar
- Outer (tensor) product: `%o%` or `outer()`, returns a matrix

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: vecTimes

# Element-wise product
# Multiply corresponding positions: (a1*b1, a2*b2, ...)
a * b

# Inner product (dot product)
# Returns a scalar: sum(a*b)
a %*% b

# Outer product (tensor product)
# Returns a matrix: A[i,j] = a[i] * b[j]
a %o% b

```

:::

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Vector \"non-standard\" operations" .fragment}

- In R, `a / b` is element-wise division; mathematically vectors have no division
- Recycling rule: the shorter vector is repeated to match the longer one

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: vectorDiv

# Element-wise division
# Divide corresponding positions: (a1/b1, a2/b2, ...)
a / b

# Verify reversibility of the operation
# Create a new vector d that is 313 times a
d <- a * 313

# Then divide d by a — every result should be 313
d / a

```

:::

---

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: recycle

# Define a short vector (length 2)
e <- c(1, 2)

a * e
```

:::

- `c()` can also create character vectors — wrap each element in double quotes

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: character

# Character vector — each element must be in double quotes
aSentence<-c("I","am","from","People's Republic of China", ".")

aSentence

```

:::

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 1**: The recycling rule

Given the assignments of `a` and `e`, explain and compute the result of the following expressions.

::: { .hint exercise="recycle_rule" }

- `a` has length 5, `e` has length 2
- R recycles `e` to `c(1, 2, 1, 2, 1)`
- Then performs the operation element-wise

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: recycle_rule
#| exercise: recycle_rule

a <- 11:15
e <- c(1, 2)

a - e
a / e

```

:::

::: { .solution exercise="recycle_rule" }

```r
a <- 11:15
e <- c(1, 2)

# e is recycled to c(1, 2, 1, 2, 1)
# a - e: c(10, 10, 12, 12, 14)
a - e

# a / e: c(11, 6, 13, 7, 15)
a / e
```

:::

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 2**: Derive the Sexagenary Cycle (Ganzhi)

Use vector operations to derive the 60-pair sexagenary cycle. Rule: Yang stems pair only with Yang branches; Yin stems pair only with Yin branches.

::: { .hint exercise="ganzhi" }

- Use `seq()` and `%%` to separate odd / even positions (Yang / Yin)
- Use `outer()` to generate all pairwise combinations
- Use `paste0()` to concatenate stems and branches into text

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ganzhi
#| exercise: ganzhi

tiangan <- c("甲", "乙", "丙", "丁", "戊", "己", "庚", "辛", "壬", "癸")
dizhi <- c("子", "丑", "寅", "卯", "辰", "巳", "午", "未", "申", "酉", "戌", "亥")


```

:::

::: { .solution exercise="ganzhi" }

```r
tiangan <- c("甲", "乙", "丙", "丁", "戊", "己", "庚", "辛", "壬", "癸")
dizhi <- c("子", "丑", "寅", "卯", "辰", "巳", "午", "未", "申", "酉", "戌", "亥")

# Extract Yang stems (odd positions) and Yin stems (even positions)
tg_yang <- tiangan[seq(tiangan) %% 2 == 1]
tg_yin <- tiangan[seq(tiangan) %% 2 == 0]

# Extract Yang branches (odd positions) and Yin branches (even positions)
dz_yang <- dizhi[seq(dizhi) %% 2 == 1]
dz_yin <- dizhi[seq(dizhi) %% 2 == 0]

# Yang stems pair with Yang branches
outer(tg_yang, dz_yang, FUN = paste0)
# Yin stems pair with Yin branches
outer(tg_yin, dz_yin, FUN = paste0)
```

:::

## Matrices

- Matrix: two-dimensional, composed of multiple vectors
- `matrix()` creates one, filling column by column (column-major order)
- Specify `nrow` or `ncol` (only one is needed)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: matrix

# A 2-row matrix; data fill column by column
matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), nrow = 2)
```

:::

---

- Matrices also follow the recycling rule

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: matrixRecycle

# A 2-row matrix
matrix(1:3, nrow = 2)

```

:::

---

- Matrix indexing: `A[row, column]`
- A blank row or column index means "all rows" or "all columns"

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: matrixCell

# Setup: build matrix A
A <- matrix(1:3, nrow = 2)

# Single cell: row 1, column 2
A[1, 2]

# Entire row 1
A[1, ]
# Entire column 2
A[, 2]
```

:::

---

- Transposition: `t()`, swaps rows and columns
- Two transpositions restore the original

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: matrixTrans

# Original matrix
A
# One transposition
t(A)
# Two transpositions (back to original)
t(t(A))
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-important title="The logic of nested function calls" .fragment}

- "Inside-out" evaluation: the innermost call runs first, its result feeds the outer call
- Analogous to mathematical function composition $g(f(x))$

:::

---

- Matrix addition / subtraction: same dimensions, element-wise
- Matrix multiplication: element-wise `*`, inner `%*%` (cols of first = rows of second), outer `%o%`, cross-product `crossprod()`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: matrixTimes

# Setup: create matrices of different dimensions
B <- matrix(1:6, nrow = 2)
C <- matrix(6:1, nrow = 2)
D <- matrix(6:1, nrow = 3)

# Inspect the matrices
B
C
D

# Basic operations (require matching dimensions)
B + C
B - C

# A tour of multiplications: element-wise, inner, outer, cross-product
B * C
B %*% D
B %o% C
crossprod(B, C)

```

:::

---

- `solve()` solves a linear system
- The matrix form of a linear system closely mirrors the matrix form of a regression

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: solve

# Coefficient matrix B (3x3) for the system
# Coefficients of a, b, c on the left-hand side
B <- matrix(c(1, 3, 2, 1, 2, 2, 1, 4, -6), nrow = 3)
B

# Right-hand side vector y
# Results on the right-hand side: 6, 9, 4
y <- c(6, 9, 4)

# Solve the linear system
# Find vector x such that B %*% x = y (i.e., the values of a, b, c)
x <- solve(B, y)
x

```

:::

## No need to remember everything in one time"

This chapter and the chapters that follow will cover quite a few concepts.
Some of them do not need to be mastered all at once ---a general impression is enough, and many will deepen through later chapters as you see them applied in context. 

Other items, however, form the key foundation of the current stage; missing them now will directly hinder later understanding.
Each chapter therefore ends with a key-point recap that flags exactly what you must hold onto at this moment.


## Recap

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Assignment: `<-`
- Boolean logic
    - Comparison operators: `==` (equal), `!=` (not equal), `>`, `<`
    - Logical operators: `&` (and), `|` (or), `!` (not)

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Vectors and indexing
    - Build a vector: `c()`
    - Generate a sequence: `seq()`
    - Vector indexing: `[]`
- Matrices
    - Create: `matrix()`
    - Inspect dimensions: `nrow()`, `ncol()`
    - Transpose: `t()`
    - Solve / invert: `solve()`

:::

::::
